name: Thutmose III
description: Thutmose III, often considered the greatest pharaoh of the New Kingdom, reigned during the 18th Dynasty. Born as a prince, he ascended to the throne alongside Hatshepsut, his stepmother and aunt, who initially ruled as regent and later as pharaoh. Upon Hatshepsut's death, Thutmose III solidified his rule and embarked on a series of military campaigns that expanded Egypt's empire to its greatest extent. He led his armies in numerous battles across the Levant and Nubia, securing vast territories and tribute. His most famous victory was the Battle of Megiddo, where he decisively defeated a coalition of Canaanite kings. Thutmose III's military prowess and administrative skills transformed Egypt into a dominant power, ushering in an era of prosperity and cultural flourishing. He commissioned numerous building projects, including temples at Karnak and Deir el-Bahri, leaving a lasting legacy as a warrior king and a patron of the arts. He is also known for systematically erasing Hatshepsut's name and image from monuments, a controversial act that historians still debate. His reign marked a high point in Egyptian history, remembered for its military achievements, economic stability, and artistic innovation. Thutmose III's strategic genius and ambition cemented his place as one of the most influential rulers of ancient Egypt.
subtitle: The Napoleon of Egypt
achievements: Expanded Egyptian empire to its largest extent
Established Egypt as a dominant power

keyMyths.title: The Battle of Megiddo
keyMyths.description: Thutmose III decisively defeated a coalition of Canaanite kings at Megiddo. He used a daring strategy to outflank the enemy, securing a major victory and control over the region. This battle cemented his reputation as a brilliant military strategist.

keyMyths.title: The Expansion of the Empire
keyMyths.description: Thutmose III led numerous campaigns to expand Egypt's empire. He conquered territories in the Levant and Nubia, bringing vast wealth and resources to Egypt. His military successes established Egypt as a dominant power in the region.

keyMyths.title: The Erasure of Hatshepsut
keyMyths.description: After Hatshepsut's death, Thutmose III systematically erased her name and image from monuments. The reasons for this action are debated, but it may have been to secure his own legitimacy or to restore the traditional male line of succession. This has been a source of controversy.

sources.author: James Henry Breasted
sources.description: A comprehensive collection of translated Egyptian texts.
sources.text: A comprehensive collection of translated Egyptian texts.

sources.author: Donald B. Redford

sources.text: An exploration of the historical interactions between Egypt and its neighbors.

associations.name: Sphinx
associations.significance: Represents royal power, wisdom, and guardianship, qualities associated with Thutmose III's reign and military prowess.

associations.name: Amun-Ra
associations.significance: Thutmose III heavily promoted the worship of Amun-Ra, attributing his military victories to the god's favor and solidifying the deity's position in the Egyptian pantheon.

associations.name: Chariot
associations.significance: Symbolizes Thutmose III's military leadership and strategic mobility on the battlefield during his numerous campaigns.

associations.name: Lotus
associations.significance: Represents rebirth and Upper Egypt, reflecting Thutmose III's efforts to restore and expand Egyptian power.

associations.name: Gold
associations.significance: Associated with royalty, divinity, and the sun, reflecting Thutmose III's divine right to rule and the prosperity of his reign.

associations.name: Kopesh Sword
associations.significance: Represents military might and the successful campaigns waged by Thutmose III to expand Egypt's empire.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both were renowned military leaders who expanded their empires through strategic campaigns and consolidated their power through political maneuvering.
Both were brilliant military strategists who led their armies to conquer vast territories and establish extensive empires.
Both were known for their effective administration and tolerance towards conquered peoples, contributing to the stability and longevity of their empires.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Building
worshipPractices.description: Thutmose III commissioned numerous temples and monuments dedicated to various gods, particularly Amun-Ra, throughout Egypt and Nubia. These served as centers of worship, economic activity, and royal propaganda, reinforcing his legitimacy and divine connection.

worshipPractices.name: Military Processions
worshipPractices.description: Victorious military campaigns were often celebrated with grand processions and festivals, showcasing the spoils of war and the captured enemies. These events reinforced Thutmose III's image as a powerful warrior and the protector of Egypt.

cultural.modernLegacy: Thutmose III is remembered as one of Egypt's greatest pharaohs, celebrated for his military achievements, administrative skills, and patronage of the arts. His reign is studied by historians and archaeologists, and his monuments continue to inspire awe and admiration.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Ascension
extendedContent.content: Born a prince of the 18th Dynasty, Thutmose III's early life was largely overshadowed by Hatshepsut, his stepmother and aunt. Upon the death of Thutmose II, Hatshepsut became regent for the young Thutmose III, but she soon assumed the full title of pharaoh, effectively sidelining him. During Hatshepsut's reign, Thutmose III received military training and administrative experience, preparing him for his eventual rule. While the exact nature of their relationship remains debated, it is clear that Hatshepsut's reign provided Thutmose III with a unique opportunity to learn the intricacies of governing Egypt. He learned military strategy, diplomacy, and the importance of maintaining religious and economic stability. Upon Hatshepsut's death, Thutmose III ascended to the throne as the sole ruler, ready to assert his authority and embark on a new era of expansion and prosperity for Egypt. His experiences during Hatshepsut's reign shaped his approach to governance and military leadership, setting the stage for his future achievements.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests and Military Campaigns
extendedContent.content: Thutmose III is best known for his extensive military campaigns, which expanded Egypt's empire to its greatest extent. He led at least seventeen campaigns into the Levant, securing control over vital trade routes and resources. His first major campaign was the Battle of Megiddo, where he decisively defeated a coalition of Canaanite rulers. This victory established Egyptian dominance in the region and opened the way for further expansion. Thutmose III's military strategies were innovative and effective, often employing surprise attacks and well-coordinated maneuvers. He personally led his troops into battle, inspiring them with his courage and determination. Beyond the Levant, Thutmose III also expanded Egyptian control southward into Nubia, securing valuable gold mines and other resources. His military achievements brought immense wealth and power to Egypt, solidifying its position as the dominant force in the region. These campaigns were meticulously documented on the walls of Karnak Temple, providing invaluable insights into his military strategies and the political landscape of the ancient Near East.

extendedContent.title: Lasting Legacy
extendedContent.content: Thutmose III's legacy extends far beyond his military achievements. He was a prolific builder, commissioning numerous temples, monuments, and obelisks throughout Egypt and Nubia. These structures served not only as places of worship but also as expressions of royal power and artistic achievement. He expanded and renovated the Karnak Temple complex, adding new pylons, halls, and obelisks. His patronage of the arts led to a flourishing of Egyptian culture, with the creation of exquisite sculptures, paintings, and jewelry. Thutmose III also reorganized the Egyptian administration, implementing reforms that improved efficiency and stability. He promoted trade and commerce, fostering economic prosperity throughout the empire. His reign marked a golden age for Egypt, characterized by military strength, economic prosperity, and artistic innovation. He is remembered as one of Egypt's greatest pharaohs, a visionary leader who transformed Egypt into a powerful and influential empire. His accomplishments continue to inspire awe and admiration, ensuring his place in history as a true ruler.

shortDescription: Thutmose III, a pharaoh of the 18th Dynasty, is celebrated for his military genius and expansion of the Egyptian empire to its greatest extent.
